content: Calories are a unit of energy measurement for heat energy required to raise one gram of water from 14.55 to 15.5 degrees Celsius at atmospheric pressure.
One-thousand calories is equal to 1 kilocalorie, or 1 kcal for short. Most people use the term "kilocalorie" and "calorie" interchangeably.
A calorie (or kcal) tells you how much energy you get by burning the food. In the "old days" this is how folks figured out the energy content of food using a device called a calorimeter.
Conventional wisdom, perhaps better referred to as Current Dogma says that you gain weight because you eat more than you expend. This is almost true!
To be 100% true, it would read: when you gain weight, it is the case that you have necessarily eaten more than you expended.
The change in our fat mass is equal to what we eat and drink (the only source of energy entering our system) less all of the energy we expend.

We expend energy in four ways: Digestion (all the energy we require to break down food, plus the undigested portions that leave our body); Exercise (everyone knows what this is, but I tend to separate it from daily activity since people really like to focus on exercise); Daily activity (the non-exercise activity we carry out); Basal expenditure (the energy we expend underlying any activity – e.g., when you are resting).
